data.title: UPS: Shrinking To Greatness?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Grab a cup of tea and sit comfortably, because this one is going to be dense.

Before we start, I want to lay it out straight: I've never liked the logistics industry as an investment, whether that be Uber (UBER), United Parcel Service (NYSE:UPS), FedEx (FDX), DoorDash (DASH), or Deliveroo (OTCPK:DROOF)

Something just doesn't sit right.

One time, it's the guilt of looking at the working conditions of delivery drivers, but at the same time, the harsh financial impacts of a well-negotiated labor union contract (such as the one negotiated by Teamsters with UPS). Speaking of UPS specifically, there are always allusions to economies of scale of a "unified network" creating "operational efficiencies", but then, we're surprised by them axing high-volume customers such as Amazon. (Note: FedEx did the same in 2019, but now appears to be in discussions to pick up the pieces left by UPS. Amazon has also relaunched a logistics service to compete with UPS and FedEx). What a complicated web of frenemies competing in a labor-intensive, thin-margined sector, where the primary differentiator is price.

Then comes the tariffs and de minimis changes, constantly changing in scope, effective dates, and rates, that it's hard to tell if the 11.3% decline in the UPS's International segment operating profit is something to celebrate as a sign of resilience in face of extremely disruptive environment or the tip of an iceberg - an omen of something more sinister to come.

I honestly don't know where to start. Perhaps an easy target? That would be calling management's bluff regarding the share buybacks, dividend increase, and insider stock purchases.

Calling the Bluff

Earlier this year, UPS highlighted significant challenges ahead. Tariffs, axing of Amazon's business, cost increases, and portfolio changes. They said they have this all under control. Then, the headline news hit the screens that they had increased their dividends. If they're increasing dividends, how bad could it be? With shares down and dividends up, I believe some thought this was an opportunity. I certainly was intrigued, which prompted this piece.

But here's the thing. I think the dividend increase is more of optics than a reflection of confidence in future prospects. Here's why. The dividends were increased by 1 cent. That's the minimum that a publicly traded company can change its dividend. Second, it translates to only a 0.6% increase, far less than the historical dividend growth rates we're accustomed to seeing.

In my opinion, management simply wanted to maintain the abstract, yet highly valued, status of a dividend contender, having increased dividend payouts for 15 consecutive years. The CEO, the first outsider to run the firm, wasn't about to drop the ball now, particularly with UPS alumina holding a significant portion of the voting power, having received Class A shares with 10 voting rights each, right before the company became public 25 years ago. Currently, Class A shares, which are not traded on a public market, constitute approximately 60% of the voting power (111.5 million Class A shares vs. 736 million Class B shares, as of June 2025 Quarterly Filing).

I have to give it to UPS; they have purchased $1 billion in shares this year. However, share repurchases have become more sporadic than in the past. As for the insider purchases, I am not sure we can draw much from that. It was just $1.5 million worth of buy transactions in late July through August across three Form 4s for 3 executives: $1 million of which was from the CEO. If I were a CEO with a multi-million-dollar compensation package, I would likely spare some if it meant a chance to instill confidence in the share price. However, it is possible that the CEO believes her stock awards do not provide sufficient exposure, given the decline in the share price. You can't really know.

In any case, and call me old-fashioned if you want, but I think the best way to assess a company's prospects is to actually look at its financials.

Is the Dividend Covered

Here's why many retail investors are examining signs such as share buybacks, dividend increases, management comments, and insider trading to evaluate a dividend. UPS financials are complex. They have derivative mark-to-market movements that impact net income, restructuring charges, and gains on the sale of assets, among other one-time items, which don't affect the core business: the engine powering dividend payments.

Here's the thing. Whatever way you look at it, UPS currently doesn't make enough to pay a dividend.

First, let's look at the GAAP payout ratio, which stood at 109%. That means that the company paid more than it made, on a GAAP basis. That's also above the historical average, and the highest in the past decade, notwithstanding the pandemic disruptions, when the company recorded more than $6 billion in mark-to-market defined pension changes due to declining rates and plummeting stock prices.

On an adjusted basis, things aren't that rosy either. Even when accounting for all these one-time items, including goodwill impairments, severance packages, and laid-off employees, the current payout ratio remains unsustainable, and equally important, it is the highest in at least ten years.

The bottom line is that these are extraordinary circumstances, and the payout levels aren't consistent with UPS's historical averages, which is why management felt it necessary to state that they're committed to paying a dividend.

Now, I know some of you will say, things usually pick up in the holiday season. Well, that's true, but they also slow down in Q3, so all in all, H1 and H2 are mostly balanced. However, even when comparing H1 2025 with historical H1s, we clearly see that the payout ratio is above historical averages. UPS is undoubtedly in an uncomfortable position.

104% Payout? Management Will Sort it, Right?

One might argue that UPS isn't deep in the red. This 4% is above the 100% payout mark, at just $115 million ($230 million annualized). Management says they're cutting expenses and raising prices. They also bought new companies. Volumes are increasing on international routes, such as Europe-India and China to the rest of the world, where UPS has hubs.

So, they will sort this out, right? Well. Here's the thing. Running a breakeven payout ratio at 100% requires things to run perfectly, and this isn't what we're observing right now.

The cost-cutting initiative? Well, it's not working as expected. Adjusted costs are still high. The $3.5 billion savings they announced hinge on things like driver buyouts from their labor union contracts, which prohibit UPS from trimming non-managerial headcount. This contradicts the "shrink to greatness" model they're currently running. USPS also stopped taking low-margin last-mile work from UPS. They want packages to go to their sorting facilities, and then to their last-mile distribution centers. Sorting facilities are where the money is, thanks to the numerous automated machines that enable economies of scale. UPS has to handle the last-mile work on its own, and it has realized that it costs a lot.

Things aren't perfect in the M&A arena either, as UPS has pulled out of the Estafeta acquisition.

But here's the kicker. Things will likely get worse. Here's why:

The de minimis exceptions for small packages ended on August 29, 2025. Its impact will bite fully in Q4, and to a lesser extent in Q3.

Second, the tariff situation is also fluid. UPS benefited from a surge in imports in the months leading up to the April Liberation Day tariffs, as importers sought to stockpile goods as a hedge against the expected increase in customs duties. That benefited Q1 2025 significantly. We saw an acceleration in volume declines in Q2 compared to Q1 2025. I believe things will worsen in H2 as consumers adjust their behavior to current tariffs and customs, particularly those related to China

Finally, UPS is axing its delivery service to Amazon (AMZN), its largest customer to make room for high-margined parcels. In my head, this is like trading a bird in hand for two in the bush. Here's my take on all of this. First, I expect EPS to decline, at least in the short term. Second, the inability to realize economies of scale from the Amazon contract highlights execution and operational challenges on a level that wasn't reflected in the share price. So, I don't see an immediate rebound potential. The price decline is justified, given the new information that this event highlights to the market about UPS's limitations.

Valuation

I don't think UPS is undervalued. Demand is shrinking. High-margin products such as healthcare and SMB businesses won't fill the gap left by Amazon and new customs rules. On an absolute basis, the forward P/E ratio of 13x is fair, especially given the weakness of the e-commerce growth exposure thesis.

Even comparatively, when excluding smaller peers, UPS trades at similar valuation multiples compared to peers. FedEx trades at a forward P/E ratio of 13x. Deutsche Post (OTCPK:DHLGY), at 12.6x. One can even argue that DHLGY is better positioned than UPS and FedEx because they have less exposure to US customs rule changes and more exposure to the booming international trade between Europe and Asia.

Summary

A lot is happening at UPS. It's undergoing a strategic transformation, shifting towards high-margin delivery services for healthcare and small to medium-sized businesses, and away from the e-commerce logistics business of Amazon (AMZN) and Temu (part of PDD Holdings (PDD)). It's a 'shrink-to-glory' type of pitch from management, but the market isn't really buying into it.

I believe that the dividends will be cut, and if not, the credit rating on UPS' debt will be downgraded. The company isn't generating enough revenue to sustain its dividend, and its growth prospects are dim.

The primary risk to this thesis is that the de minimis rule changes are diluted or tariffs are struck down by the Supreme Court. The healthcare segment is also growing rapidly, both organically and through mergers and acquisitions, which is a tailwind. UPS is also well-capitalized, with $6.3 billion in cash, which it could use to offset the dividend deficit until conditions improve. Our view is that the risks outweigh the rewards here.

data.title: Porsche Automobil Holding SE: Lower Guidance, So We Are Still Neutral
data.description:
After our recent note on Exor’s half-year results (with a buy rating confirmed), we return to the automotive space with a focus on Porsche Automobil Holding SE’s (OTCPK:POAHF)(OTCPK:POAHY)—particularly in light of Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG’s €1.8 billion profit warning. As in previous updates, we review the latest results before outlining our neutral stance in light of the most recent developments. For our new readers, Porsche SE is an investment holding company with two main assets: a 53.1% stake in Volkswagen AG ordinary shares and a 25% stake in Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG ordinary shares. Since our last update (March 2025), the share price has remained broadly unchanged, effectively validating our previously stated neutral view.

Fig 1

HY Results And Our Neutral Stance

Earnings from Volkswagen and Porsche AG are reflected in Porsche SE’s financial result under net income from investments. So, the H1 adj. group result reached €1.1 billion (Fig. 2) and was mainly impacted by lower profits. Indeed, Volkswagen AG and Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG reported profit before tax of €1.2 billion and €100 million, respectively. This compares to last year's results of €2 billion and €300 million. So, the company's results after tax reached €300 million. As a direct consequence, the company has revised downward its full-year guidance for the adjusted group result after tax, now expecting it to fall within a range of €1.6 billion to €3.6 billion, down from the previous estimate of €2.4 billion to €4.4 billion.

Source: Porsche Automobil Holding SE H1 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still neutral?

  1. Porsche Automobil Holding SE downgrade reflects a softer market backdrop. Meanwhile, the group’s net debt target remains unchanged. At June-end, net debt reached €4.9 billion, slightly down from €5.2 billion by 2024. The absence of debt reduction (Fig. 4) could point to either a new investment on the horizon or a possible outflow related to ongoing litigation;
  2. Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG's product strategy realignment included the indefinite pushout of future BEVs, which resulted in a €1.8 billion negative. This issue will positively impact future depreciation and amortization, but it goes deeper into a failure strategy with massive investment over the last ten years. This write-down won't be calculated for the 2026 dividend payment. So, we still project a Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG DPS of around €2.5/€3;
  3. Regarding dividends, we anticipate DPS from Volkswagen AG and Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG will likely decline to around €1 billion in 2026. This level comfortably covers the expected dividend payout of around €600 million, assuming a stable DPS of €1.91 (Fig. 3). That said, this provides no additional upside. However, Wall Street DPS consensus is estimated at €2.35, so there might be pressure on a lower shareholders' return;
  4. During the Q&A analyst call, the company’s management indicated it is exploring adding a third core portfolio holding. There was no specific timeline provided. With approximately €2.6 billion in cash and cash equivalents (including Securities and Time Deposits), Porsche Automobil Holding SE has ample capacity to pursue a sizable acquisition. Still, this is much lower than Exor's current firepower (estimates of €4 billion in potential acquisitions). While the automotive sector remains relevant (and more pronounced than Exor, given our view of Ferrari as a pure luxury player), management noted that infrastructure, software, and defense opportunities are also being evaluated. Any such transaction, they emphasized, would need to contribute to and diversify the company’s dividend income stream. So, again, this is more related to value over growth prospects. This is too early for us; at this stage, no new investment has been factored into our current forecasts. However, NAV growth could be limited given the value investment opportunity;
  5. On a valuation basis, as reported in our Exor coverage, the Agnelli holding "benefits from a stronger cash position, broader diversification, lower leverage, and, significantly, a higher pace of portfolio asset rotation." That said, Porsche Automobil Holding SE's current discount to NAV is 15%, while the Exor discount is 53%. We believe this valuation gap is not aligned.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Changes In Earnings and Valuation

Following the company's results, we believe the current cash flow and the dividend will be covered. In our estimates, we also consider the ample headroom to cover annual interest expenses of approximately €250 million. Despite the guidance reduction, the company's valuation is a sum of the parts of the main holdings. Porsche Automobil Holding SE has 156.693 million shares in Volkswagen and 113.875 million shares in Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG. This represents an enterprise valuation of €19.2 billion. Considering €5 billion of net debt and no litigation, our equity value reached €14.2 billion. Continuing to apply the Exor discount to the NAV (35%), we value the company with a target price of €30.5 ($3.5 in ADR). Our neutral view is confirmed.

Risks

Downside risks of Porsche Automobil Holding SE include: 1) ongoing litigation, 2) execution risks of new investment, 3) share price fluctuations of Volkswagen and Porsche AG, 4) changes in dividend policies of the two main holdings, 5) lower earnings diversification, 6) FX change, and 7) complexity in the company's governance. We should also include market risks to which VW and Porsche AG are exposed.

Conclusion

Following the H1 results and considering the latest developments at VW and Porsche AG, we still prefer Exor's value proposition. While the holding discount might be subject to, Exor holding, broadened its earnings base and is well-positioned for higher growth returns.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cintas: Valuation Is Too High For My Appetite
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a hold rating for Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ:CTAS). Solid execution is real, and 1Q26 does show healthy organic growth, margin expansion, and business model resilience. Strategic levers such as converting “no-programmers” and cross-selling into an existing base of customers remain powerful growth drivers. That said, the weakness in Uniform Direct Sale, combined with broader macro uncertainties, highlights that risks are still present. Given the current valuation at ~40x forward PE, I believe the risk-reward is unattractive with little margin of safety.

Company Description

CTAS is the largest uniform services provider in North America, serving businesses across a wide range of industries. The business is organized into four segments, led by Uniform Rental and Facility Services, which represented 77% of FY25 revenue. This core segment provides rented uniforms and a suite of ancillary products like mats, towels, and cleaning chemicals. First Aid & Safety (12% of revenue) supplies first aid cabinets, training, and AED maintenance, while Fire Protection Services (8%) focuses on inspection and servicing of sprinklers, alarms, and emergency signage. The remaining 3% comes from Uniform Direct Sale, which sells apparel outright to large strategic accounts.

1Q26 Earnings Review

1Q26 revenue came in at $2.72 billion, up 8.7% y/y, with acquisitions contributing 0.9%. Adjusting for that, organic growth was 7.8% y/y. By segments, Uniform Rental and Facility Services saw 7.3% y/y organic growth, while First Aid & Safety saw a robust 14.1% y/y growth. Fire Protection also performed well, growing 10.3% y/y organically. The only drag was Uniform Direct Sale, which fell 9.2% y/y due to the lumpiness of large programs. Profitability trends were equally healthy. Gross margin expanded by 20 bps to 50.3%, reaching $1.37 billion, with Uniform Rental contributing most of the lift as segment margins improved 40 bps to 49.7%. Operating income grew 10.1% y/y to $617.9 million, pushing operating margin up 30 bps to 22.7%. As such, net income was $491.1 million, up 8.7% y/y, and adj. EPS was $1.20, representing a 9.1% y/y growth.

Resilient Business Model

At a high level, the main positive takeaway I had was that the CTAS business model is very resilient. Despite labor market pressures, CTAS was able to continue to show solid organic growth and was driven by a strategic lever—conversion of “no-programmers” that management can repeatedly pull to sustain organic growth for the foreseeable future. These “no-programmers” are essentially businesses that historically managed uniforms and facility needs in-house. The value proposition for outsourcing these functions is compelling, as CTAS helps customers meet their image, safety, cleanliness, and compliance needs. For instance, management highlighted a recent conversion of a Department of Transportation that was struggling with the time and effort of administering its own safety vest program, along with budgeting difficulties and inconsistent employee compliance. By implementing a full-service rental program, CTAS provided a solution that reduced administrative burdens, offered more predictable budgeting, included the convenience of a laundry service, and ultimately improved safety compliance among workers.

This allows clients to save time and money and refocus their efforts on their own customers. Because of the CTAS scale, its greater route density, purchasing power, and process efficiency allow it to better leverage its fixed costs, which can translate into cost savings for the customer.

That aside, CTAS also has a deep pool of customer base (>1 million customers) that it can continue to cross-sell additional services to grow organically without adding new logos. The cadence is straightforward. Once CTAS lands an initial business with a customer, CTAS will focus on delivering a good customer experience and becoming a trusted resource over time. This, then, opens the door to providing more solutions. One good example is a long-term manufacturing client that only used the CTAS Uniform Rental program. As this client was expanding and its team became overwhelmed with the workload, they looked to CTAS for help. CTAS was then able to add its Facility Service line, First Aid and Safety Services, and Fire Protection Services to the client’s new building. If you take a step back, it makes a lot of sense to simply outsource more non-critical functions to CTAS if one can free up resources. For instance, mats, mops, restroom supplies, first aid cabinets, fire extinguishers, and sprinkler system inspections are all non-critical in nature (for the business growth) but are important to meet regulatory compliance. Doing so not only diversifies CTAS revenue streams within each account but also significantly increases customer stickiness, making the overall revenue base more visible and less susceptible to the macro cyclical pressures.

Mini red flags and macro concerns

Despite the strong headline numbers, a deeper look at CTAS 1Q26 results shows several underlying dynamics worth noting. The Uniform Direct Sale segment, for example, reported a 9.2% y/y organic revenue decline. While this is CTAS's smallest business, representing just 2.6% of revenue, it was still able to create a 30 bps drag on consolidated growth in the quarter—a mini red flag, I believe. But what is more important is that this segment carries strategic weight beyond its revenue size. Direct sales often serve as an entry point into large national accounts, particularly in hospitality. Winning a “front of the house” apparel contract can pave the way for up/cross-sell opportunities in, say, first aid and fire protection, areas with more lucrative recurring revenue. As such, this decline, while attributed to lumpiness, is something to monitor. If growth stays negative for the next few quarters, it could indicate softer new service wins in other segments.

The macro backdrop is also something investors should ignore (notwithstanding how resilient the business model is). As I mentioned, there are internal organic levers CTAS can pull to drive growth, but the broader business is inherently tied to macro cycles—in bad times, businesses fire employees, which means lesser demand for uniforms; businesses may also close down, which affects services for fire alarms, inspections, cleaning equipment rental, etc. We have also seen in the past that during downturns, organic revenue growth turned negative. I believe the US macroeconomy is still very uncertain. While the Feds have chosen to cut rates, the labor market is showing softness, and tariff impacts have yet to really hit consumers' balance sheets. I would think it is too early to determine if the US economy is out of the woods for now; hence, it would be safe for investors to remain cautious in this aspect, as a broader economic slowdown could still materially impact CTAS volume and growth trajectory.

Valuation

Anyway, even if I was totally wrong about the headwinds, CTAS valuation is just too high to justify a buy rating. There are 3 main players in this industry: CTAS, Unifirst (UNF), and Vestis Corp (VSTS). CTAS trades at ~40x forward PE, while the other two trade at 19x and 17x, respectively. The bulls will argue that CTAS is a significantly larger business (~$10.5 billion revenue vs. peers with ~$2.5 billion revenue on average), and because of that, it has a much better scale advantage, which can be seen in the margin difference (CTAS with 28% EBITDA margin vs. UNF with 14% and VSTS with 11%). I recognize this fact, and I think it makes sense for CTAS to trade at a premium. The question is how much of a premium makes sense. At 40x forward PE, the market is implicitly valuing CTAS as a better business than Microsoft (MSFT)—which has a better revenue growth outlook (mid-teens vs. CTAS at high-single-digits) but only trades at 33x forward PE. This doesn’t make sense to me. The bear case here is that if CTAS fails to deliver the growth expectations, which can be easily triggered due to macro pressures, the market may revise multiples downwards sharply. In other words, the valuation has very little margin of safety.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for CTAS. While CTAS' scale, high-margin model, and execution clearly justify a premium, the current valuation leaves little margin of safety. The combination of potential macro pressures, softer performance in smaller but strategically important segments, and an elevated multiple relative to peers and even mega-cap tech makes it difficult to recommend chasing the stock here. In my view, investors are better served by waiting for either a more attractive entry point or clearer evidence that CTAS can sustain outsized growth relative to expectations.

data.title: Snowflake: Not For The Faint Of Heart
data.description:
As shown in the below chart, I haven't been particularly bullish on Snowflake Inc. (NYSE:SNOW) despite the stock's run up. In June, I upgraded to a hold rating but note that was due to technical strength. In terms of the fundamentals, the setup wasn't great as guidance was soft and the stock was trading at a large premium. Since the publishing of that article, the stock has gone on to gain by around 6%.

In the below analysis, it is shown that the long term outlook for Snowflake is bright. Furthermore, Q2 financials showed notable improvement for the company and they also raised full year guidance. However, the valuation is a major deterrent in my view. Snowflake's massive premium to the IT sector seems excessive even if future potential is high. That said, with the company being one of the most promising in the cloud computing space, I have decided to retain my hold rating on the stock.

Big Picture

While AI is just one of Snowflake's product categories, I believe it has quickly become one of the most important for the company. When taking a big picture view, Snowflake is clearly seeing major strength in this space. In the above visualization, AI Data Cloud sharing activity is shown for the respective dates. Back in 2020, AI in the cloud was basically non-existent and now take a look at the activity for this July. Absolute stunning how the cloud computing landscape has changed in the past few years. Clearly, AI will be a key driver for Snowflake in the years or even decades ahead.

Above is a chart showing the potential growth of Snowflake's overall TAM. This isn't specific to their AI business but shows the opportunity ahead. Back in calendar year 2024, TAM was just $170 billion but by calendar year 2029, it is expected to more than double to $355 million. In addition to AI, the world is in the age of data. With leading data engineering and analytics solutions, Snowflake seems set to capitalize on this important field as well. Of course, AI and data are intertwined and given the strong AI growth shown above, I believe the company has become even better positioned in the data side of the business. Now, let's take a look at their most recent financial results.

Demand Is Strong

Snowflake reported their FY2026 Q2 earnings near the end of August and top line results were encouraging. The company saw total revenues of $1.145 billion, up 31.78% YoY. As you can see above, it seems that top line growth has hit an inflection point as the YoY growth rate has rebounded rather sharply from the previous quarter. After many quarters of deceleration, Snowflake has finally bucked the trend and that shows demand may be picking up meaningfully. Total revenues were also able to beat expectations by $57.49 million and so there was fairly healthy outperformance in the quarter. For product revenues specifically, they saw 32% growth to $1.09 billion. Overall, these headline figures show robust business activity.

The substantial increase in RPOs is another sign that demand is strong. The balance of RPO in Q2 was $6.932 billion, up 33% YoY. While that is a mild slowdown of growth from Q1's 34% increase, I believe this nonetheless demonstrates that businesses are essentially lining up to be Snowflake's client. Also shown above is the fact that cRPO as a percentage of total RPO has stayed relatively steady over the course of the past five quarters. This shows that Snowflake is expecting to be able to convert a large amount into revenues in the near future.

As a signal of Snowflake providing unmatched value, the company's net revenue retention rate has stayed well north of 100%. In Q2 the metric was at 125%, representing an increase from Q1. It is modestly lower than in the prior year period but as a whole, all of the quarters shown above reflect solid demand. Not only is Snowflake able to keep its customers, the company is able to attractive clients to spend more over time. Clearly, their AI and various data tools are of incredible value to enterprises as they are coming back for more continuously.

Profitability Improves

As a whole, the company's profitability showed respectable improvement. Adjusted product gross margin did not change YoY but a figure in the mid 70s percentage range is quite strong. As for adjusted operating margin, there was significant expansion from 5% to 11%. This is a major jump in corporate efficiency. Looking at their income statement, you can see that general & administrative expenses increased by just 22.2%, far slower than top line growth. With revenue growth rebounding and operating margin expanding, that is a recipe for strong EPS growth and that is indeed what we saw in Q2.

Snowflake reported an adjusted EPS of $0.35, representing 94.44% of YoY growth. That is a significant acceleration from the previous quarter's 71.43% and so overall profitability of the company seems to be on quite a good trajectory. Lastly, if you look at the adjusted FCF margin chart above, you can see that there was some contraction. Personally, I don't find this to be too concerning since their purchases of property and equipment increased by 230% YoY. The company is still investing for the future and so some cash flow weakness is to be expected.

Full Year Outlook Raised

In their Q2 earnings release, Snowflake provided some updates for full year guidance and also provided a Q3 specific outlook. We'll start with the full year numbers. For product revenue growth, they have raised their FY2026 expectations from 25% to 27%. This is of course a positive direction but note that in FY2025, product revenue growth was 30% and so it may take some time for the growth re-acceleration to show up in the longer time frame. For operating margin, they raised their expectations from 8% to 9% and so corporate efficiency will be better than they thought. Furthermore, this will be a notable improvement from the previous fiscal year's 6% margin and so profitability is clearly picking up. Overall, the upward revisions for full year guidance are definitely promising for investors.

Now for Q3, they see product revenue growth in the range of 25% to 26%. That is a bit disappointing as it would represent a deceleration from Q2's 32%. Therefore, in the near term, it still may not be completely smooth sailing for the company. As for operating margin, they are projecting 9%. This is also a notable expansion from 6% in FY2025 Q3 and so at least profitability is still headed in the right direction. In my view, the slowdown in top line growth is a step back but given the strong demand metrics that we discussed earlier, I believe this will be just a short term issue.

Valuation Is Excessive

While the forward P/E ratio may not be at one of its highest levels relative to historic levels, I believe a reading of 188.27 is not for the faint of heart. Firstly, I started this article with a big picture view and it was shown that the long term direction and outlook for the business is strong. AI is a strong growth driver and strong demand metrics show that their products are creating real value for customers. In their Q2, there were some promising signs in their financials and as stated above, full year guidance for key metrics was raised. Up until the valuation assessment, I was encouraged by what I was seeing.

Now, while the fundamentals are solid, is the growth rate of the company really able to justify the valuation? As of this writing, Seeking Alpha shows that Snowflake's non-GAAP forward P/E is at a 628.36% premium to the IT sector. That is simply eye-watering. The reason I believe this high of a valuation may not be justified currently is because growth is only decent and an operating margin of around 10% is really nothing to get excited about. Sure, the long term outlook is bright but at these prices, it would be a rather risky bet. Therefore, I wouldn't consider current levels in the valuation to be attractive.

Conclusion

Snowflake's business seems to have a great future ahead but that doesn't necessarily mean that the stock is a buy. As stated above, the long term outlook is strong with AI and data analytics being core growth drivers. However, at a forward P/E premium of over 600%, I believe investors are basically pricing in perfection or even more. For overvaluation, I usually slap a sell rating on stocks but given the company's strategic position within cloud computing and the growth potential over the next few years or decade, I have decided to be a bit more neutral on the stock. With that in mind, after today's analysis, I'm reiterating my hold rating on Snowflake.

data.title: Celsius Holdings: Alani Nu Is The Real Game Changer - Further Upgrades Likely
data.description:
CELH Remains Attractively Valued Despite the Recent Rally, Thanks to Its Renewed Growth Prospects From Alani Nu Acquisition

We previously covered Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) in May 2025, discussing how the Alani acquisition had been highly strategic indeed, attributed to the latter's high growth/profitable cadence, along with the accretive dollar share growth.

Combined with the growing international sales, the regionalized manufacturing capabilities, the renewed growth boost after the painful inventory correction, and the cheap valuations, we had believed that the stock was likely to offer a double-digit upside potential then.

CELH 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating, CELH has outperformed with a +64.6% rally by its August 29, 2025, peak, before returning some of those gains by the time of writing, which resulted in a +33.1% net gain compared to the wider market at +14%.

These numbers remain promising indeed, attributed to the renewed growth opportunities arising from the Alani Nu acquisition, as observed in the combined company's FY2024 revenues of $1.95B and FY2024 net income before provision for income taxes of $264.78M, and the subsequent growth to FQ2'25 annualized levels of $2.95B and $517.84M, respectively.

This is especially since Alani Nu has sustained its robust FY2024 revenue growth from $594.9M (+27.9% YoY) and net income performance from $69.74M to annualized FQ2'25 levels of $1.2B and $374.4M, respectively.

Readers must also not forget CELH's growing energy drink US share of 16.8% in H1'25 (+5.8 points YoY) at the expense of Monster Beverage's (MNST) declining share to 26.8% (-1.3 points YoY) and the accretive nature of its adj EBITDA margins to 28.4% in FQ2'25 (+3.5 points YoY) "despite incorporating Alani Nu's lower-margin profile."

As a result of these robust developments, we can understand why the market has turned bullish on the energy drink company's growth prospects, as observed in the recent stock price outperformance.

These reasons may also be why PepsiCo (PEP) has expanded CELH's distribution deal while the latter "takes on the key strategic role of energy lead for PepsiCo in the U.S., overseeing strategy for Celsius, Alani Nu, and Rockstar Energy," as they seek to consolidate their energy drink portfolios to an overall energy drink US share of 19.7% as of FQ2'25 (+2.2 points from H1'25 levels of 17.5%) while growing their sales and consumer base moving forward.

This is a relevant development indeed, since it allows CELH to increasingly tap into the booming domestic energy drink market growth from $25B in 2024 to $37.9B in 2030, as observed in the company's accelerated unit sales growth of +31.2% in FQ2'25 compared to the industry-wide growth of +13.5% YoY over the same time period.

Therefore, while CELH may have reported an impacted balance sheet with a net debt level of $250.68M in FQ2'25, compared to the net cash position of $977.28M in FQ1'25 and net cash position of $903.21M in FQ2'24, we are not overly concerned indeed.

This is because the Alani Nu acquisition is likely to drive the combined company's renewed top/bottom-line growth opportunities right from day one, significantly aided by the growing free cash flow generation of $35.46M in FQ2'25 (+16.5% YoY) and the relatively low net-debt-to-annualized-FQ2'25-adj-EBITDA ratio of 0.29x, compared to the sector median of 1.32x.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result, we can understand why the consensus has tentatively raised their forward estimates, with CELH expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +38%/+45.3% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +17.9%/+20.1% a year ago (pre-Alani Nu acquisition) and the 4Y historical growth at +79.5%/+105.4%, respectively.

At the same time, based on CELH's growing adj EPS from FY2024 levels of $0.89 (including Alani Nu) to the annualized FQ2'25 adj EPS performance of $1.88 and annualized H1'25 adj EPS performance of $1.30, we believe that the company is likely to well exceed the consensus' conservative FY2025 adj EPS estimates of $1.16 as well, with an estimates upgrading very likely upon another double beat performance in FQ3'25.

CELH Valuations

For now, with the raised consensus forward estimates well balancing the outsized stock price gains, we believe that CELH still looks attractive at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 27.96x, not too far from our last Buy article levels of 21.85x and the 1Y mean of 22.80x.

Based on the current stock price of $51.34 and the annualized FQ2'25 adj. EPS of $1.88 including Alani Nu, CELH still boasts a relatively reasonable LTM P/E non-GAAP valuation of 27.30x as well, compared to the 1Y mean of 39.25x and the 5Y mean of 194.21x.

On the one hand, when comparing it to its energy drink/beverage peers, including:

  • PEP at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 13.11x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 17.67x,
  • Coca-Cola Company (KO) at 19.80x/22.30x,
  • Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) at 11.44x/13.26x, and
  • MNST at 24.41x/33.63x, respectively,

It is apparent that CELH's rally may have occurred relatively fast and furiously, with it triggering its somewhat premium valuations compared to its peers.

On the other hand, assuming that CELH is able to sustain the high-double digit adj. EPS growth of +46% between the FY2024 levels and the annualized H1'25 levels, we believe that it remains compellingly valued as a high-growth profitable stock at our estimated FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.59x, compared to the sector median of 2.68x and its peers, including:

  • PEP at 4.49x,
  • KO at 3.96x,
  • KDP at 1.90x, and
  • MNST at 2.23x.

So, is CELH Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

CELH 5Y Stock Price

For now, CELH's uptrend support line has been observed along the 50-day moving averages since early 2025, with the higher highs/higher lows highlighting the returning bulls after the much-needed growth opportunities arising from the Alani Nu acquisition.

Assuming that the combined company is able to consistently deliver the robust adj. EPS growth profile at +46% moving forward, we are looking at an extremely robust FY2027 adj. EPS estimate of $2.77.

Combined with our estimated P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.30x, it is apparent that CELH still offers a robust upside potential of +47.2% to our bull-case long-term price target of $75.60, despite the high double-digit stock price recovery thus far.

CELH's Bottoming Technical Indicators

Thanks to the recent -19.1% correction from the August 2025 heights, CELH now appears oversold based on the Relative Strength Index [RSI] as it retests the prior October 2023 support levels of $50s by the time of writing.

With the stock already nearing its established uptrend support line, we believe that the bulls may soon come to defend a floor near the current levels and subsequently, its upward recovery from henceforth, significantly aided by the moderating short interest volume by -14.9% on a YoY basis.

As a result of the promising technical indicators, the double-digit upside potential, the renewed growth opportunities from the Alani Nu acquisition, and the improved margin of safety from the recent pullback, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the CELH stock here.

Do not miss this dip.

data.title: Merck: 25% Total Return CAGR Potential
data.description:
Company Background

Merck & Co. (NYSE:MRK) is a $65B in sales company with manufacturing capabilities in prescription medications, vaccines, biologic therapies and animal health products. The company has extensive R&D (Research and Development) capacity especially in oncology, with over 60 oncology trials currently.

It’s leading drug (and the top selling drug in the world), Keytruda, generated over $29B in sales in 2024 and is expected to continue its growth with patent expiration not until 2028, having increased sales by 18% Y/O/Y (year/over/year). The company is a leader in both oncology and vaccines, including six of the top 20 vaccines by sales in 2024. Top seller, Gardasil generated over $8B of revenue in 2024, but it has come under pressure in 2025 with sales falling 55% during the first half of 2025 primarily attributed to falling demand in China and Japan for the HPV (human papillomavirus) vaccine. Revenue growth in other products and cost-reduction efforts have mostly offset the earnings dip caused by Gardasil in 2025.

Second quarter earnings beat expectations by $0.10 per share but were down Y/O/Y by about 7%, coming in at $2.13. Keytruda sales increased by 9% compared to the same period of last year. Revenue was down by 1.5% from last year, primarily due to the fall in sales of Gardasil. Animal Health sales rose by 11% compared to the same period of last year. Full year EPS (Earnings Per Share) guidance is for $8.87 - $8.97 per share compared to consensus of $8.87. My estimates are $8.95/share for 2025 and $9.70 for 2026.

You may find the quarterly earnings presentation from Merck very informative.

I will get into prospects for future growth in a later section.

Industry Outlook

There is a lot of uncertainty around the potential future impact of tariffs, but the good news is that the impact thus far has been far less than expected. President Trump has commented that he could raise tariffs on pharmaceuticals to as high as 250%, but I believe this, as was the case with many other pronouncements, is a negotiating stance more than a real threat. I’ve been right so far on my expectations on the tariffs front (see my earlier article, “The U.S. Economy in Five Years”). Thus far, more companies, especially pharmaceutical manufacturers, have committed to invest $Billions in building new manufacturing capacity in the U.S. over the next four years. I think that those who follow through with those commitments will avoid most, if not all, tariffs.

Other concerns in the industry are related to uncertainty in policy related to vaccines and potential drug pricing regulations. Once again, I think these issues have been overblown in the news media, to some extent to create political friction, but primarily to attract readers with shocking headlines. Most vaccines will still be available and many will continue to mandated, but some, like Covid vaccines, will become optional, in my opinion.

I think that politicians are going about drug pricing all wrong because they are looking at the problem of high prescription drug costs in the U.S. in a vacuum. The problem is a more global one, in my opinion, and it should be approached as such. I have outlined a book on the subject to propose a viable solution to the problem and plan to publish early 2026. There is a common-sense approach that will reduce the cost of drugs in the U.S., but so far our leaders have not stumbled upon it. In the end, I think there will be too much dissension to what will be proposed by the administration that we will most likely see gridlock rather than legislation in the near term.

This industry has been out of favor due to all the uncertainty, but it has been gaining some traction in recent months. I believe that, as the fog of uncertainty lifts and the future becomes more visible, the industry will once again outperform the broader market. I think that selective investments in pharmaceuticals will pay off handsomely for patient, long-term investors with an investing horizon of five years or longer.

Dividend

Merck has raised its dividend for 14 consecutive years and at an average pace of nearly 7% over the last five years. The current yield is ~4% and the payout ration is a very comfortable 41.4%.

I expect dividend growth to continue at about a 6.5% CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) over the next five years as EPS rise even faster (more about that in a later section). The expected growth rates in the table below shows forward growth rates that should support additional dividend increases, especially when we look at FCF (Free Cash Flow) and Return on Assets expected growth rates.

Merck maintains a solid balance sheet and remains very profitable. Its pipeline has multiple multi-billion-dollar potential candidates and I foresee a continuing healthy rise in dividends over at least the next five years.

Profitability

Merck’s profitability score of A+ pretty much sums up what I expect to see continue for the foreseeable future. The company’s R&D, combined with strategic acquisitions and collaborations, are set to continue generating profitable growth.

I’ll discuss the pipeline in the next section, but the acquisitions and collaborations shown above demonstrate that the company is not standing still and resting on its past accomplishments. Management is constantly assessing potential growth targets for further development, either internally or with partners.

Growth Prospects

Merck’s Keytruda is the world leader in sales. Patent protection ends in 2028, so much of that nearly $30B in revenue will need to be replaced. Gardasil was the world’s leading vaccine by sales, as mentioned above, but is losing market share at a rapid rate.

Management expects its recent approvals of injectable forms of Keytruda by the U.S. FDA (Food and Drug Administration) and by the EU drug regulator, the EMA (European Medicines Agency) to lead to additional sales of Keytruda and a transition of 30-40% of patients from the IV (intravenous) administered treatment to injections within the first 18 months of launch.

The advantages of the injectable Keytruda include the time to administer falling from more than 30 minutes to as little as 1-2 minutes. That is a decided convenience for both patients and medical professionals. It will also make the treatment available to more patients by reducing the strain on hospitals providing the intravenous treatments and making treatment available at a broader range of medical facilities. It does not, however, extend the patent protection.

Merck has several treatments in various stages of trial, using Keytruda as a foundation, combining it with other compatible drugs to improve outcomes. Such combinations, if/when approved, could extend the patent protection in the new forms which should retain much of the market share if proven more beneficial than the original Keytruda.

The growth grades shown below are encouraging even if they are mostly based upon historical evidence. I believe that the EPS Forward long-term growth (3-5 CAGR) of 9.46% is low by my estimates. My expectation is for EPS of 13% CAGR over the next five years. My forecast is higher due to the company’s potential share repurchases and significant growth from new drugs coming from the pipeline and through acquisitions.

The pipeline is brimming with potential blockbusters, especially in the oncology area. That is important to people like myself, since I had a cancerous tumor removed a year ago and am cancer-free so far. I’m at that age (76) when cancer tends to become more prevalent, so I applaud Merck’s attempts to find new ways to treat cancer. My brother (90 years old) is also a survivor. One of my sisters and a sister-in-law died from cancer. My family has some experience with the disease.

Cancer touches a large segment of families, so much so that it is hard to find many people who do not have a relative who has been inflicted by it. The addressable market is huge.

The coming acquisition of Verona Pharma has added a potential multi-billion-dollar blockbuster to the pipeline. The purchase price of $10B is, I believe, going to look like a steal in a few years.

When you consider the potential of treating ~4.3 million patients annually when the distribution network of Merck is leveraged, the potential is staggering. But Verona, without Merck’s network and manufacturing capacity would probably have never been able to reach the full potential of the drug it had brought to market.

When we extrapolate the average annual cost per prescription ($71.3 million / 25,000) of $2,852 to the full expected eventual patient count of 4.3 million, I calculate the potential annual revenue to be ~$12B. That probably won’t be fully attained until sometime in the early 2030s, but it is just one of the many potential blockbuster drugs that Merck will be launching or ramping up over the next five years.

In addition to the COPD (Chronic Obstruction Pulmonary Disorder) entry from the Verona acquisition, as mentioned earlier, Merck has over 60 drug trials in various stages for oncology, with expected potential future sales of ~$25B, but that is only part of the picture. The company has several new drugs in its pipeline for other indications. HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) is another area with great potential for Merck to address. As you can see from the slide below, the addressable market is $26B today, and expected to grow to $34B by 2035. The company has four treatments in its pipeline, one of which is submitted for approval and expected to be available in 2026.

All totaled, my expectation is for Merck’s current pipeline to generate ~$50B in new revenue by 2035. And the company has a history of adding multiple new promising candidates each year along with future acquisitions. I believe that will more than offset any losses in revenue from patent expirations and lead to continued growth for the foreseeable future.

Merck vs Peers

Merck met my criteria that I use to define a quality company in 9 out of 10 metrics and also scored well on my FCF (Free Cash Flow) value metric. Here is how it scored relative to my metrics (including industry averages). You can find a more detailed comparison of my top tier pharmaceuticals companies in my analysis of the industry here.

Merck’s yield of 4% is 67% above the industry average and my expected CAGR in dividend is 6.5%, well above my desired 5% minimum. The payout ratio is comfortably below the industry average and very manageable.

Revenue & Earnings Growth

Merck’s future expected growth in revenue is below the industry average but my expected future growth in EPS significantly outpaces the industry average, so I am willing to accept the lower revenue growth because the company has the ability to generate a higher margins from each dollar in sales. Merck’s operating margin is above the industry average now and I expect this ratio to improve in the coming years. The company has initiated a new cost reduction program to further improve margins.

Capital Structure

Merck has no goodwill or intangible assets to speak of, meaning that it has not overpaid for acquisitions. That is a sign of good asset allocation by management. Its debt to assets ratio is significantly below the industry average providing Merck with greater flexibility in its capital structure compared to the average company in the industry. Its ROTC (return on total capital) is measurably higher than the industry average meaning that Merck’s management has consistently made superior asset allocation decisions in the past and I expect the trend to continue.

Free Cash Flow
